text.title: Value Proposition Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Value Proposition Design application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Both canvas sides MUST be complete: customer (jobs/pains/gains) AND offer (products/relievers/creators). One-sided canvases are rejected.
rule.rationale: A one-sided canvas is a feature list or a wishlist; the diagnostic value is in the pairing.

rule.statement: Every pain MUST be paired with ≥1 pain reliever; every gain MUST be paired with ≥1 gain creator. Unpaired pains/gains are rejected (or marked as gaps).
rule.rationale: Unpaired pains = unaddressed value; unpaired gains = wasted offer surface. Pairings are the fit check.

rule.statement: Customer-side entries MUST cite the JTBD transcripts they came from. Made-up pains are rejected.
rule.rationale: A canvas authored from imagination misses the actual customer; JTBD grounding keeps it honest.

rule.statement: The positioning statement MUST follow Geoffrey Moore format: "For [segment] who [need], [product] is a [category] that [benefit]. Unlike [alternative], we [differentiator]." Deviations are rejected.
rule.rationale: A consistent format makes positioning statements comparable across products and stable across rewrites.

rule.statement: The canvas MUST address exactly one primary segment per artefact. Multi-segment canvases are split.
rule.rationale: Segments have different jobs/pains/gains; a multi-segment canvas conflates them and produces generic copy.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
